text:
**Produced** by `extract_open_questions()` (`capture.rs:2075`), which runs at the *end* of every interactive/daemon capture (it's explicitly skipped in archeologist bulk mode). For each captured session it: [...] - reads the wiki index + the **last 8 000 chars of the transcript** (`build_open_questions_transcript`),
- asks the triage model: *"list up to 8 nouns/named concepts used in this conversation that are NOT in the wiki index; skip generic words"* (`capture.rs:2105`),
- dedups by `slug` and **appends** to `open-questions.json` (`capture.rs:2159`). [...] **1. It's a self-perpetuating loop.** The injected `<open-questions>` block becomes part of the *new* session's transcript. That transcript later gets captured, and `extract_open_questions` sees those same nouns "used in the conversation" but still "not in the wiki index" → re-extracts them. I traced the earliest transcript mention of "xyzzy" — and it's *itself* an injected `<open-questions>` hook block, not real prose. So the question re-seeds itself every session. It only dies when a guide file with the exact slug gets created. [...] **2. The extractor is ungrounded — it never reads the code.** It only sees transcript text + the wiki index. So any noun *mentioned* (or injected) gets flagged as "missing," regardless of whether it exists in reality. `xyzzy frobnicator` — the canonical nonsense canary (Colossal Cave + frobnicate) — appears nowhere in `src/`; it almost certainly was seeded once as a test (or to probe whether a session blindly obeys injected instructions), and the loop in #1 has kept it alive ever since. [...] **3. The file is append-only, slug-deduped, never pruned.** That's why it's grown to 74 KB. Wrong or stale questions accumulate; the *only* removal path is creating a matching wiki guide. [...] So: these aren't coming from your 253 conversations describing real undefined concepts — they're a mix of genuinely-undefined nouns (chunker, embedder, PostToolUse hook, agents.db — those are real) and **self-reinforcing noise** that the pipeline can't distinguish, because it reasons over transcript text + index instead of the actual codebase. It's the same root weakness as the sparse-wiki issue: the capture pipeline isn't grounded in the code. [...] Worth fixing — candidates:
- **Ground extraction in the code** (grep the noun in `src/` before flagging; if it doesn't appear anywhere, drop it).
- **Strip injected `<open-questions>` blocks** in `strip_harness_xml` so they don't feed back into extraction (breaks the loop).
- **Prune on injection**: TTL or a "asked N times, never answered → retire" rule, instead of pure slug-dedup append.
